calls_processed.email_html:
ACTION REQUIRED — Review & Send

Draft follow-up for Ryan (ryan@themagicconcepts.com).
Segment: SMB / Emerging  |  Call: SiteZeus <> Wing It North (Wing Stop Franchisee)

Review below, edit as needed, then send from abenton@sitezeus.com.
Do not reply to this message — it was generated automatically.

Suggested subject: Great connecting — SiteZeus resources & next steps

Hi Ryan,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your goal of growing from roughly 80 to 200 Wingstop locations and the core challenge standing in the way: because your business runs at roughly 60% third-party delivery, traditional white space and impact models don't fully capture whether a new site will cannibalize an existing one or open up genuinely new demand.

Our impact analysis uses mobile data to track customer behavior across existing locations and model transfer risk at potential new sites — and while we do capture some third-party delivery traffic through that mobile data, I want to be straightforward with you: we don't have a dedicated DSP radius tool, and neither does anyone else right now given how tightly Uber Eats and DoorDash guard that routing data. That said, if your team ever wants to see how our impact and forecasting models perform against your existing predictive model in a market like Detroit or Long Island, I'm happy to set up a deeper demo.

Relevant Resources

  • Sales Impact & Cannibalization — Ryan's primary concern was understanding delivery-driven cannibalization when opening stores in dense markets like Queens and Long Island — this speaks directly to that challenge.
  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — Ryan is already using mobile data and Placer; this shows how SiteZeus's mobile data sourcing compares and how it informs trade area analysis in dense, delivery-heavy markets.

Thanks,
Emma Keeney

Hi Paul,

Below is a summary of the action items discussed during SZ | 30 minute call - Paul Terry and Alan May.

Quick Call Review

  • Paul and Greg confirmed they're staying on — good momentum and growing comfort with the scorecard and whitespace data.
  • Identified and resolved the psychographic data gap: the AGS Feb 26 project in SiteZeus has the persona/segmentation data needed for the scorecard export.
  • Scorecard structure agreed: two separate 100-point scores (demographic and segmentation) rather than a blended 200-point total — spreadsheet logic updated live on the call.
  • Eden Prairie (MN) flagged as a strong site (100 on segmentation score); cannibalization discussion tabled for a future conversation.

Action Items

SiteZeus Team (Alan May)

  • Send Paul and Greg the updated scorecard spreadsheet with demographics weighted at 25/25/25/25 as a starting point and segmentation section separated out cleanly.
  • Ensure the AGS Feb 26 project is the working project going forward so psychographic/persona data exports correctly.
  • Plan to rebuild the active model and pull updated persona live data as part of a mid-year revenue update (roughly 1-2 months out).

Customer Team (Paul Terry, Greg Trzcinski)

  • Review the updated scorecard and finalize the demographic variable weightings (household net worth, quality of life, white-collar index, owner-occupied housing units).
  • Check out the new platform (app.sitezeus) using the AGS Feb 26 project to explore the in-platform scorecard scoring on pins.

Key Decisions

  • Scorecard will be structured as two independent 100-point scores — one demographic, one segmentation — rather than a single blended score.
  • Demographic variables confirmed: quality of life index, average household net worth, white-collar in combination, and owner-occupied housing units.
  • Model rebuild will be deferred and bundled with a mid-year revenue update to avoid disrupting the current well-performing model.

Best,
Alan May

calls_processed.email_html:

Segment: SMB / Emerging  |  Call: SiteZeus Locate <> Watershed Car Wash

Review below, edit as needed, then send from mitchg@sitezeus.com.
Do not reply to this message — it was generated automatically.

Suggested subject: Great connecting — SiteZeus resources & next steps

Hi Scott,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

You shared that Watershed is targeting two new openings this year with three to four per year going forward, focused on secondary and tertiary markets across Texas, Oklahoma, and neighboring states — largely because of the competitive pressure Quick Quack and Club Car Wash are creating in the major MSAs. The priority question your private equity partners keep raising is what tool you're using for white space, and you mentioned Placer AI fell short there.

Our white space analysis is built exactly for what you described — scoring thousands of addresses across your target states, filtering out anything below an 85, and surfacing the underserved cities and corridors where Watershed has the strongest opportunity. We'd layer in foot traffic data on competitors like Quick Quack to show you where they're performing best and where the gaps are. I'll send over a few resources below that are relevant to where you are in the process.

Relevant Resources

  • White Space Analysis Guide — Scott's primary use case is identifying underserved secondary markets across a four-to-five state region before committing to new sites.
  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — We discussed geofencing Watershed locations and using mobile and credit card data to profile customers and benchmark competitor foot traffic like Quick Quack.
  • Customer Stories — Scott mentioned needing better answers for private equity — a relevant client result could help support that internal conversation.

Thanks,
Mitch Gunderson

Suggested subject: Great connecting — SiteZeus resources & next steps

Hi Mike,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your team's primary goal of giving CSCS franchisees a data-backed way to respond when Dine Brands announces a dual-brand development in their trade area — specifically around understanding cannibalization and sales transfer before or during a dispute. You mentioned that access to franchisee-level sales data may be limited given Dine's public company status, so I want to confirm that the cannibalization model works independently of that; transfer percentages are calculated from mobile data alone, and any revenue impact can be applied on your end using your own numbers.

I'll put together two pricing scenarios as we discussed — one focused on cannibalization and core GIS functionality as a starting point, and one that layers in broader location intelligence and predictive modeling. I'll also outline what a go-live plan could look like so you and Melanie have a clear picture of the path forward.

Next Steps

  • Will to send two pricing scenarios: (1) cannibalization and core GIS only, (2) full location intelligence and predictive modeling add-on
  • Will to draft a proposed go-live plan for review
  • Determine feasibility of accessing historical publicly disclosed performance data as a workaround for the revenue forecasting model

Relevant Resources

  • Sales Impact & Cannibalization — Directly supports the primary use case discussed — giving franchisees data to evaluate or dispute dual-brand development impact in their trade area.
  • Franchise Growth Webinar — Relevant given the franchisee co-op structure and the dual-brand development committee David Paradise is chairing on behalf of the franchise community.

Thanks,
Will Morgan

Hi Cwelch,

Below is a summary of the action items discussed during SZ | 60 minute call - Coleman Welch and Alan May.

Quick Call Review

  • Cwelch's CEO needs a board-ready one-pager by Thursday explaining how SiteZeus forecasts work — Alan committed to delivering a concise, non-technical summary by end of day tomorrow.
  • Identified that the current model is missing a few stores and has some site attribute gaps; months open and store type are the two data points that will meaningfully improve model accuracy.
  • Agreed to simplify the next model rebuild by dropping low-signal attributes and only keeping the variables Cwelch can actually provide clean data for.
  • Franchisee name and agreement type turned out to have negligible signal, so neither needs to be prioritized for the rebuild.

Action Items

SiteZeus Team (Alan May)

  • Send a concise, board-ready one-pager (or short deck) explaining the SiteZeus AI model and how forecasts/cannibalization numbers are derived — end of day tomorrow at the latest.
  • Include a separate snapshot of current model accuracy and top attributes so Mike can decide whether to share it with the board.
  • Clone the current model, drop low-signal attributes, and rebuild once months open data is received from Cwelch.

Customer Team (Cwelch)

  • Pull and send months open data for all store locations (requires CRM export and reconciliation — flagged as the top priority).
  • Confirm store type (plant vs. drop) for the locations currently marked as 'plant' to clarify their status in the model.

Key Decisions

  • Only mature stores (with at least 12 months of sales data) will be included in the model rebuild.
  • Low-signal site attributes (ingress visibility, signalized intersection, Google reviews, franchisee name, agreement type, etc.) will be dropped from the next model version.
  • The board one-pager will be kept high-level — focused on inputs, AI methodology, and outputs — with model accuracy detail provided separately for Mike to include or exclude at his discretion.

Next Milestone

  • Alan to deliver the board one-pager and model accuracy snapshot to Cwelch by end of day tomorrow, ahead of the Thursday board meeting.

Best,
Alan May

Hi Juan,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your goal of expanding three distinct concepts — Dirty Rabbit, Tacos Atarantados, and Sala de Especho — each requiring a very different location profile and customer base. You shared that today the process relies on a mix of broker insight, gut feel, and paid demographic pulls, and that you want a more reliable tool to complement that as you scale, particularly as Sala de Especho demands a latino community with the right income profile and Dirty Rabbit needs a specific indoor/outdoor setup.

SiteZeus can help you build a data-driven profile for each concept using the customer data you already have across your locations, then weight the demographics, psychographics, and co-tenancy factors that actually drive performance for each brand. That foundation sets up a white space analysis to prioritize markets as you grow outward from South Florida into Orlando and beyond.

Next Steps

  • Schedule a tailored demo — no specific date confirmed on the call, follow up to lock in a time

Relevant Resources

  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — Luis emphasized needing to validate that a latino community in a target market also has the right income profile — exactly the consumer and demographic intelligence this covers.
  • White Space Analysis Guide — The team described a hub-and-spoke expansion strategy starting in South Florida and moving outward, making white space identification a direct next step.
  • Restaurant Expansion Planning — With three concepts targeting 25+ locations over five years across multiple states, a structured expansion planning framework is directly relevant.

Thanks,
Will Morgan

Hi Tyler,

Below is a summary of the action items discussed during Daily Sync - SiteZeus | Fresh Kitchen.

Quick Call Review

  • Walked through the scorecard together with Tyler Azzarelli (broker) in the loop — good alignment on how the demographic scoring logic works and why some locations score the way they do.
  • Identified that the current customer profile segments cover ~42% of trade area population on average, and the 50% benchmark we set is aggressive — there's room to calibrate.
  • Miami stood out as a clear outlier driven by City Hopefuls over-indexing and low Urban Singles presence, plus site-specific factors (structured parking, visibility) — not purely a demographics story.
  • Agreed that adding Suburban Boomers and/or Sunset Boomers to the segment mix could close the gap toward the 50% population threshold across the portfolio.

Action Items

SiteZeus Team (Aaron Bergland)

  • Add Suburban Boomers (and evaluate Sunset Boomers) to the scorecard segment mix and show how it shifts scores across the full portfolio.
  • Show Tyler McRae the mobile trade area view that was demoed yesterday — he couldn't locate it on his own.

Key Decisions

  • The 50% population threshold for a perfect demographic score is intentionally aggressive — the team is open to adjusting it to match the actual portfolio average (~42%) if the current bar doesn't produce meaningful differentiation.
  • Suburban Boomers will likely be added to the target segment list; Sunset Boomers are under consideration but not a primary target.

Next Milestone

  • Daily huddle cadence is ongoing — next session will review the updated scorecard with boomer segments added.

Best,
Aaron Bergland

Hi George,

Below is a summary of the action items discussed during SZ | 30 minute call - David Denker and Alan May.

Quick Call Review

  • Identified root cause of model inaccuracy — wide revenue range (sub-$300K to $1.5M+ stores) and non-traditional locations (casinos, kiosks, airports) are distorting forecasts.
  • Agreed on a clear path forward: rebuild model with a $500K revenue floor and remove Vegas casino locations to establish a meaningful baseline.
  • Nashville store flagged as a live test case — model forecasts ~$1M while current actuals are trending up 20-25% post-takeover, giving a real-world accuracy check.
  • SiteZeus Atlas scorecard functionality introduced as a potential alternative/complement to predictive modeling for future site evaluation.

Action Items

SiteZeus Team (Alan May)

  • Rebuild the model excluding sub-$500K revenue sites and Vegas casino locations — targeting delivery tomorrow or the following day.
  • Run a standalone forecast on the Nashville store (kept out of model but pinned separately) and share results.
  • Run Site Sonar nationally (up to 50,000 addresses) using agreed brand locations and export forecast results.
  • Export full demographic data (all ring/drive-time bands) for all RMCF locations, including non-traditionals, and deliver to the team.
  • Send George and Jess the full list of brands available in Site Sonar for their selection.

Customer Team (George Bria, Jess Soditch)

  • Work with Alan to pull and leverage all available data exports from the SiteZeus platform before contract end.

Key Decisions

  • Rebuild model with a $500K revenue floor — all sub-$500K sites removed.
  • Vegas casino locations (Orleans and one other) excluded from the model due to non-traditional, tourism-driven nature.
  • Nashville store excluded from the model rebuild but will be individually forecasted as a real-world accuracy benchmark.
  • Team agreed to reconnect in 3-6 months to evaluate whether the refined model and outputs held up against actual performance.

Next Milestone

  • Informal check-in in 3-6 months to review whether the rebuilt model and Site Sonar outputs aligned with actual store performance.

Best,
Alan May

Hi Chad,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your goal of selecting the remaining nine franchise sites within the next 90 days and making that process more efficient for you personally, since you're still heavily involved in every market tour. You mentioned that your current process — using population data, zip code sizing, and boots-on-the-ground brokers — has worked well enough to get you to 17 open locations, but a couple of underperformers showed that raw demographic data alone doesn't always tell the full story.

That's exactly where we can help. By analyzing your existing store portfolio alongside your Square customer data, we can identify which specific customer segments are actually driving success for Millie's — similar to the work we did with Kilwins — so that when you're flying out to tour sites, you're only spending time on locations that are genuinely worth your while. I'll send over a follow-up demo so you can see the full platform walkthrough we started on your 232 South Highland Ave location.

Next Steps

  • Send full platform demo recording or schedule a follow-up demo to complete the walkthrough started on the 232 South Highland Ave location

Relevant Resources

We discussed your goal of building a site selection consultancy — Unity Partners — starting with friends and family looking to open Indian restaurants and coffee shops in the Nashville area, with a longer-term vision of scaling that practice regionally and eventually nationwide.

Based on where you are today, the honest fit is a bit early for a full SiteZeus subscription, since our model works best when tied directly to a brand with existing location data. That said, the path that tends to work well for consultants in your position is bringing SiteZeus in as the brand's vendor — they hold the contract, you get access, and the data stays tied to their entity. As you build out those brand relationships, I am happy to stay in touch and revisit what a tailored setup could look like. I will also keep you posted if we introduce any usage-based options down the road.

Relevant Resources

  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — We specifically discussed using mobile geofencing data to understand where customers come from and how to profile trade areas for the Nashville coffee shop and Indian restaurant opportunities.
  • White Space Analysis Guide — You mentioned wanting to identify underserved pockets — Indian restaurants in downtown Nashville and coffee shops in growing suburbs — which is exactly what a white space analysis is designed to surface.

Thanks,
Will Morgan
